title: MIBE Excel Corner
text:
Welcome to the MIBE Excel Corner! This page is dedicated to all the key concepts, notes, and resources you need for mastering the basics of MS Excel in the Business Analytics with Excel course. Whether you’re just starting or refining your skills, you’ll find everything you need.
There are two types of pages here: - Class Notes containing explanation of the main concepts, links to official documentation, and use cases. - Exercise Instructions containing videos and step-by-step instructions to practice each week’s topics.


The skills presented in this portion of the class form the foundation of effective Excel usage for business students and professionals. Mastering these fundamental techniques enables users to work more efficiently with data, reducing time spent on repetitive tasks while improving accuracy and presentation quality.



Excel proficiency is essential in modern business environments where data-driven decision-making dominates. The skills demonstrated in these videos address core business needs: data collection and organization, numerical analysis, visual presentation, and efficient workflow management. Business students who master these techniques gain the ability to analyze financial data, create professional reports, manage large datasets, and communicate insights effectively through visual formatting.




The techniques covered in this class dramatically reduce manual work. Instead of typing the same data 25 times or formatting cells individually, you will learn to leverage Excel’s built-in automation features. These time-saving methods will allow you to focus on analysis and interpretation rather than data entry and formatting.




Beyond efficiency, these skills enable users to create visually appealing, easy-to-interpret spreadsheets. Conditional formatting and data bars transform raw numbers into visual insights that stakeholders can quickly understand. Professional formatting enhances credibility and makes complex data more accessible to non-technical audiences.
Let’s dive in and sharpen those Excel skills!

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
text: Microsoft Excel is the most widely used data-processing application in the world, and its influence in organizations continues to grow. There is a good reason for this as Excel is extremely powerful - if you know how to use it. In all but a few professions your employer will expect you to be comfortable analyzing data using Microsoft Excel. If you join any kind of business your Excel skills will set you apart - for better or worse! You are taking this course to become a sophisticated end-user. Thus, mastery, rather than familiarity, should be your goal. Mastery should be your focus for two reasons. Philosophically, if you are going to do something you should do it well. Pragmatically, you realize that the competencies you acquire in Excel will very much influence the level of success you will achieve in your career. Let’s roll up our sleeves and get started!

title: Lesson 1 - Working with Workbooks
text:
Tasks and Steps

Open a new Excel workbook.
Copy only the top 25 pizza companies from “PizzaToday”

Make sure to include the header row of the table.

Recommended: Use Chrome browser for copying.

Place the data into Sheet1 of the workbook, starting from cell A1.

title: Lesson 1 - Working with Workbooks
text:
A workbook is the main file in Excel that contains one or more worksheets. When you open Excel, you’re working with a workbook, typically saved with an .xlsx extension. Think of a workbook as a binder that holds multiple pages (worksheets) of data.
Official documentation: Basic tasks in Excel




A cell is the basic unit of a worksheet, defined by the intersection of a column and a row (e.g., A1, B5). Cells can contain text, numbers, or formulas.
Official documentation: - Cell basics - Create or change a cell reference - CELL function




Rows run horizontally (numbered 1, 2, 3…), columns vertically (labeled A, B, C…). The intersection point is a cell.
Official documentation: Refer to Rows and Columns




Ctrl+C copies selected content; Ctrl+V pastes it. Works across Excel and most software.
Official documentation: Keyboard shortcuts in Excel




Hyperlinks are links in cells that point to websites, files, or workbook locations. Remove them by right-clicking → “Remove Hyperlink”.
Official documentation: Remove or turn off hyperlinks




AutoFill extends data series (like numbers, dates) by dragging the fill handle (corner of a cell). Excel recognizes patterns and continues sequences.
Official documentation: Fill data automatically in worksheet cells




Copies formatting from one cell/range and applies to another. Single-click for one use; double-click to keep active for multiple uses.
Official documentation: Use Format Painter




Font formatting changes text appearance. Ctrl+B makes text bold. Font size increases/decreases text size.
Official documentation: Format cells




Excel stores dates as numbers, displaying them based on chosen format (Long Date, Short Date, Custom). Use Ctrl+1 to format.

Official documentation: - Format numbers as dates - Number format codes




Ctrl+S saves the workbook. If new, prompts for file name/location.
Official documentation: Save your workbook




Paste Special gives advanced paste options: values only, formulas, formats, and more. Access via Ctrl+Alt+V or right-click.
Official documentation: - Paste Special - Paste options




Adds a range of cells, e.g., =SUM(A1:A10). Can also sum multiple separate cells or ranges.
Official documentation: SUM function




AutoSum (Σ) quickly inserts SUM (and other functions) into a selected cell, automatically detecting the range. Shortcut: Alt+=
Official documentation: Use AutoSum to sum numbers




A sheet is a single page in a workbook. Tabs at the bottom of Excel let you organize/use multiple sheets in one file.
Official documentation: - Select worksheets - Insert or delete a worksheet




Relative references (e.g., A1) shift automatically when formulas are copied to new locations.
Official documentation: Switch between relative, absolute, and mixed references




Absolute references (e.g., $A$1) stay fixed when formulas are copied. Add $ before letter or number as needed.
Official documentation: Switch between relative, absolute, and mixed references




The bar above the worksheet shows/edit cell content or formulas, even if the cell displays a result.
Official documentation: Overview of formulas in Excel




Calculates the arithmetic mean of specified numbers/range.
Official documentation: AVERAGE function




Add lines around cells to improve visual organization (no effect on calculations).
Official documentation: Apply or remove cell borders




Displays values as percentages by multiplying by 100 and adding %. Use % button on the ribbon.
Official documentation: Format numbers as percentages




Organize files in folders, use clear names, move from Downloads, and save frequently.
Official documentation: Save your workbook




Apply color, icons, or data bars to cells automatically based on rules (e.g., highlight values >100).
Official documentation: Use conditional formatting




Conditional formatting presets to highlight cells >, <, =, text contains, or duplicates.
Official documentation: Use conditional formatting




Conditional formatting rules can be managed, edited, or cleared using the Rules Manager.
Official documentation: Manage rule precedence




Visually represent values using horizontal bars inside cells—a quick way to compare values.
Official documentation: Use data bars




Highlight top/bottom items, top/bottom %, above/below average.
Official documentation: Use conditional formatting




Highlight repeated values within a range—useful for identifying data entry errors or duplicates.
Official documentation: Use conditional formatting




Locate and replace text or values across cells/workbook. Shortcut: Ctrl+H.
Official documentation: Find or replace text and numbers




Use formatting, colors, and graphics to make data patterns and trends visible for quick insights.
Official documentation: Use conditional formatting

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
text:
Microsoft Excel is the most widely used data-processing application in the world, and its influence in organizations continues to grow. There is a good reason for this as Excel is extremely powerful - if you know how to use it. In all but a few professions your employer will expect you to be comfortable analyzing data using Microsoft Excel. If you join any kind of business your Excel skills will set you apart - for better or worse! You are taking this course to become a sophisticated end-user. Thus, mastery, rather than familiarity, should be your goal. Mastery should be your focus for two reasons. Philosophically, if you are going to do something you should do it well. Pragmatically, you realize that the competencies you acquire in Excel will very much influence the level of success you will achieve in your career. Let’s roll up our sleeves and get started!



Be efficient – Get stuff done quickly
Be effective – Use the right skills for the right tasks Skills
Create new Excel workbooks and work on multiple worksheets
Insert data in Excel and perform basic calculations
Format cells according to their contents
Use absolute, relative, and mixed referencing
Use conditional formatting
Find and replace content in your worksheet




Being efficient in Excel will save you a significant amount of time over the duration of your career. If you have ever opened Excel you know that the application presents a big canvas of rows and columns to you. This is both a blessing and a curse. On the one hand, you have full control and lots of freedom to perform your analysis. On the other hand, as with any programming task, there many different ways to perform an analysis and it is critical that you spend time up front designing an efficient approach to it. That means that learning Excel is not just about learning where to click, rather it is about learning how to create structured solutions to analytical problems.
Insights

It is likely that if you ask ten different people to perform an analysis in Excel, they will come up with ten different procedures to achieve it. Such variability is possible because Excel provides an incredible number of features – most of which basic users will never hear about. Efficiently using the available instruments in Excel is critical. While you will develop your unique style as an analyst, efficiency should always drive the design of your workbooks. As a general rule, you want to perform analyses that are parsimonious and dynamic.

Your analysis is parsimonious when you minimize the number of calculations and activities.
Your analysis is dynamic when your workbook is not specific to your data but it is robust to data changes. That is, it will not stop working abruptly if the data changes.

A critical differentiator between sophisticated and unsophisticated Excel users is the habit to always use keyboard shortcuts. The earlier you pick up this habit, the better it is. Here are the shortcuts for your platform. Mac Win
There are some important functionalities that are only accessible through shortcuts. In addition to keyboard shortcuts, Excel offers plenty of mouse shortcuts such as auto-fill series and auto columns adjustment. Over the following sessions, we will highlight the most important ones. However, as a general rule, any time that you find yourself performing a repetitive task (like manually input the days of the week) find out the efficient approach. We guarantee you that it exists!
Creating an Excel worksheet is like programming. The idea of a ‘variable’ is central. Variables are the backbone of any program and application, and Excel is no exemption. In simple terms, a variable is a way to store information for later use. Every cell in Excel is basically a variable. You reference it by its column name and row number (e.g., A3).




In your professional life, you will often be asked to perform analyses to share with your employees, colleagues or the boss. Creating professional documents is not only visually appealing, but it is also critical for communication. Creating professional documents is critical to convey the results of complex analyses. Well-formatted, professional spreadsheets get your point across – and they look good doing it! As the designer of the workbook you will have great flexibility, it will be your call whether to present data as a plain table, a colorful one, or a compelling graph. Remember at all times that the design choices you make when sharing your analysis will impact your reader’s opinion of your content (and of your abilities).
Insights

Once you share a report with a colleague or boss you lose control of it. It might happen that, especially if it is an important analysis, your colleagues will share it themselves with other colleagues, and your results might end up in a PowerPoint presentation of a different department. If you are good at what you do, then many people will see that. If you are not that good, well, they will know.
Ultimately your peers will judge you based on the quality of what you produce. This means that you need to deliver reliable analyses presented in a manner that is easily understandable by others. Note that, while a quality presentation does not make up for errors in your data and results, a poor presentation devalues the best of insights. Don’t be your own worst enemy by doing sloppy work or by using the wrong instruments.
You might still think that Excel is just an instrument to perform calculations. In this course, you will see that Excel is much more. It is an instrument for ‘Data Storytelling’ and persuasion. When you enter the job market, as a knowledge worker, you will be expected to explain, enlighten, and engage your audience by providing compelling ‘data driven’ stories.




Objective In this module, we start using Excel. Before learning its advanced functionalities, it is necessary that you are efficient and effective with the basics. To give continuity to the learning we will use an ongoing scenario.
Scenario The idea came to you last semester. As you were cramming hard for your finals, the though hit you – Pizza Delivery by Drones, that could be a real business! You don’t know how it came to you. Maybe it’s because you had a passion for airplanes since you were a kid. Maybe it’s because you have owned all of the DJI quadcopters since the Phantom 1. Or maybe just because you were very hungry and had very little time to spare during finals week. You even have a name in mind for your firm: Papa Drones.
Over the holiday break you saw your older cousing Anna, who is a partner at a large San Francisco based venture capital firm. You told her you wanted to ask your Information Systems professor for some help, but she told you that no serious person would listen to you before you did some preliminary analysis. Anna was intrigued by the idea and was willing to help you a bit in her spare time. She said:
“if you are serious about this idea Microsoft Excel is the instrument of choice to do the analysis.”
“Of course!” you responded.
In this module, we start using Excel. Before learning its advanced functionalities, it is necessary that you are efficient and effective with the basics. To give continuity to the learning we will use an ongoing scenario.

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
text: Microsoft Excel is the most widely used data-processing application in the world, and its influence in organizations continues to grow. There is a good reason for this as Excel is extremely powerful - if you know how to use it. In all but a few professions your employer will expect you to be comfortable analyzing data using Microsoft Excel. If you join any kind of business your Excel skills will set you apart - for better or worse! You are taking this course to become a sophisticated end-user. Thus, mastery, rather than familiarity, should be your goal. Mastery should be your focus for two reasons. Philosophically, if you are going to do something you should do it well. Pragmatically, you realize that the competencies you acquire in Excel will very much influence the level of success you will achieve in your career. Let’s roll up our sleeves and get started!

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
text:

Be efficient – Get stuff done quickly
Be effective – Use the right skills for the right tasks Skills

Insert data in Excel and perform basic calculations
Format cells according to their contents
Use absolute, relative, and mixed referencing
Use conditional formatting

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
section: Be Efficient - Get stuff done quickly
text:
Be Efficient - Get stuff done quickly
Being efficient in Excel will save you a significant amount of time over the duration of your career. If you have ever opened Excel you know that the application presents a big canvas of rows and columns to you. This is both a blessing and a curse. On the one hand, you have full control and lots of freedom to perform your analysis. On the other hand, as with any programming task, there many different ways to perform an analysis and it is critical that you spend time up front designing an efficient approach to it. That means that learning Excel is not just about learning where to click, rather it is about learning how to create structured solutions to analytical problems.
Insights

It is likely that if you ask ten different people to perform an analysis in Excel, they will come up with ten different procedures to achieve it. Such variability is possible because Excel provides an incredible number of features – most of which basic users will never hear about. Efficiently using the available instruments in Excel is critical. While you will develop your unique style as an analyst, efficiency should always drive the design of your workbooks. As a general rule, you want to perform analyses that are parsimonious and dynamic.

Your analysis is parsimonious when you minimize the number of calculations and activities.
Your analysis is dynamic when your workbook is not specific to your data but it is robust to data changes. That is, it will not stop working abruptly if the data changes.

A critical differentiator between sophisticated and unsophisticated Excel users is the habit to always use keyboard shortcuts. The earlier you pick up this habit, the better it is. Here are the shortcuts for your platform. Mac Win
There are some important functionalities that are only accessible through shortcuts. In addition to keyboard shortcuts, Excel offers plenty of mouse shortcuts such as auto-fill series and auto columns adjustment. Over the following sessions, we will highlight the most important ones. However, as a general rule, any time that you find yourself performing a repetitive task (like manually input the days of the week) find out the efficient approach. We guarantee you that it exists!
Creating an Excel worksheet is like programming. The idea of a ‘variable’ is central. Variables are the backbone of any program and application, and Excel is no exemption. In simple terms, a variable is a way to store information for later use. Every cell in Excel is basically a variable. You reference it by its column name and row number (e.g., A3).

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
section: Be Effective - Get stuff done right
text:
Be Effective - Get stuff done right
In your professional life, you will often be asked to perform analyses to share with your employees, colleagues or the boss. Creating professional documents is not only visually appealing, but it is also critical for communication. Creating professional documents is critical to convey the results of complex analyses. Well-formatted, professional spreadsheets get your point across – and they look good doing it! As the designer of the workbook you will have great flexibility, it will be your call whether to present data as a plain table, a colorful one, or a compelling graph. Remember at all times that the design choices you make when sharing your analysis will impact your reader’s opinion of your content (and of your abilities).
Insights

Once you share a report with a colleague or boss you lose control of it. It might happen that, especially if it is an important analysis, your colleagues will share it themselves with other colleagues, and your results might end up in a PowerPoint presentation of a different department. If you are good at what you do, then many people will see that. If you are not that good, well, they will know.
Ultimately your peers will judge you based on the quality of what you produce. This means that you need to deliver reliable analyses presented in a manner that is easily understandable by others. Note that, while a quality presentation does not make up for errors in your data and results, a poor presentation devalues the best of insights. Don’t be your own worst enemy by doing sloppy work or by using the wrong instruments.
You might still think that Excel is just an instrument to perform calculations. In this course, you will see that Excel is much more. It is an instrument for ‘Data Storytelling’ and persuasion. When you enter the job market, as a knowledge worker, you will be expected to explain, enlighten, and engage your audience by providing compelling ‘data driven’ stories.

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
text:
Exercise Overview
Objective In this module, we start using Excel. Before learning its advanced functionalities, it is necessary that you are efficient and effective with the basics. To give continuity to the learning we will use an ongoing scenario.
Scenario The idea came to you last semester. As you were cramming hard for your finals, the though hit you – Pizza Delivery by Drones, that could be a real business! You don’t know how it came to you. Maybe it’s because you had a passion for airplanes since you were a kid. Maybe it’s because you have owned all of the DJI quadcopters since the Phantom 1. Or maybe just because you were very hungry and had very little time to spare during finals week. You even have a name in mind for your firm: Papa Drones.
Over the holiday break you saw your older cousing Anna, who is a partner at a large San Francisco based venture capital firm. You told her you wanted to ask your Information Systems professor for some help, but she told you that no serious person would listen to you before you did some preliminary analysis. Anna was intrigued by the idea and was willing to help you a bit in her spare time. She said:
“if you are serious about this idea Microsoft Excel is the instrument of choice to do the analysis.”
“Of course!” you responded.
In this module, we start using Excel. Before learning its advanced functionalities, it is necessary that you are efficient and effective with the basics. To give continuity to the learning we will use an ongoing scenario.

title: Lesson 4 – Visualize Sort and Filter Data
text: In this chapter we delve deeper into the many functionalities of Microsoft Excel and cover data visualization through charts, data sorting and filtering. Humans are a visual species, thus it is often simpler for us to absorb data in visual rather than tabular form. Excel has very well developed features for visualizing data through charts, we will explore those features. Filtering and sorting are activities we perform to “reduce” data. For these tasks Microsoft Excel requires tabular data in input and the output of sorting and filtering is also tabular data.

title: Lesson 4 – Visualize Sort and Filter Data
text:
Concepts

Input-Process-Output
Information Presentation

title: Lesson 4 – Visualize Sort and Filter Data
section: The Input-Process-Output Model in Excel Design
text:
The Input-Process-Output Model in Excel Design
In this practice assignment we reiterate a central concept in computing: Input-Process-Output. Information systems are designed to collect, process, store and distribute information. When you design your Excel workbooks you should conceptualize them as software applications. You should carefully think about the Input, Process and Output elements of your design. It often helps, as you do this, to start the design from the output. In other words, you should envision what your audience will see and how they should interact with your analysis – the presentation of your insights. With a clear idea of the output in mind you can work backward to determine what processing you need to do on your data to achieve your objectives. Notice that the input-process-output model does not imply that you must use only one worksheet for each element. In fact, as you become more and more sophisticated in your Excel skills and you tackle increasingly complex analyses in Excel, you will find it useful to have multiple sheets devoted to input data. You will also find is easier to segregate different computations on different sheets (by type of analysis for example), and you may need more than one sheet for results. The general structure and basic input-process-output design will always apply. As you conceptualize your Excel workbooks based on this model, keep in mind the following tips:

Segregate and protect your data. It is very easy to introduce errors in your workbooks when you have a lot of data – e.g., 3,000 rows. The reason is that at that scale it is impossible for you to visually inspect your data and make sense of it. So, you should always consider protecting, and even hiding, the data worksheet(s) and reference the raw data from your computation worksheets. If you need to update or change your raw data you can certainly do that, but protecting the worksheet will ensure that you (or one of your users) don’t modify data by mistake.
You may be tempted to perform quick filters or visualizations on the raw data. This may be ok if you are just trying to “get a feel for your data.” However, once you are ready to design and implement your workbook, start by segregating and protecting the data.
Maintain a single source of truth (SSOT). This requires that each data element is stored in one, and only one location. If you segregate and protect your data you are on your way to achieving SSOT. Having a SSOT ensures that when your data is updated, or if at any time you need to correct any errors in the data, the change will be automatically and reliably propagated through your whole analysis. If you are not careful to maintain a SSOT you can never be 100% sure that your analysis is correct and up to date.
Never “hard code” values in functions or computed cells. If you type numbers in a function the only way to change that number when needed, it is to modify the function in the formula bar. This is another case where you could introduce errors. And of course, if someone needs to use your workbook (e.g., your boss) you don’t want them to mess with your algorithms and your computations! The solution is to use Excel the way it was intended to be used – with relative and absolute cell referencing. You should build all the Excel’s functionality around references to cell locations.
A possible exception to the above rule is when you have constants, values that never change. However, even in that case, it is probably best to put these values in a cell – perhaps in a separate sheet called “parameters.” The advantage of doing so is that while these values may indeed never change, they are made explicit and easy to identify. This approach will eliminate problems associated with hard-coding and help your spreadsheets remain dynamic, even as they grow in complexity. It will also enhance the readability and accessibility of your workbook.

Insights

The Input-Process-Output model
Single source of truth (SSOT)
Segregating and protecting raw data
Eliminating hard coded values

title: Lesson 4 – Visualize Sort and Filter Data
text:
Information Presentation
In many ways, the visual presentation of your data is an act of storytelling. When designing a chart, you should ask yourself: “what’s the story I am communicating to my audience?” The answer to this question should guide your design choices for turning facts and figures into informative and professional charts. In a previous concept, you learned about readability and accessibility as they relate to the design of high quality Excel workbooks. The same concepts apply to charts. As you know already, charts should be self-contained. But there is more to it. Even though Excel offers many predefined chart types and themes, you should think twice before simply selecting something that “looks cool”. Color, for instance, is a powerful element to increase readability, but it is a double-edged sword because it can be very distracting, When you are designing your charts, you should ask yourself:

How are you going to present your charts? Will you be projecting your analysis during a meeting? Will you be printing your charts and handing them out? Will you be the only one using the results on your own laptop? Or will you share the file with coworkers? These questions are important because a color palette that looks good on a digital support might not be readable on paper, or when projected during a Power Point presentation. Who is the intended audience of your presentation? Is your boss’ colorblind (I for example am slightly colorblind)? It may sound trivial, but Excel provides, by default, colorblind-friendly color palettes. The general rule is simple, be cautious when expressing your artistic side, you are not painting the next Picasso, you are trying to present the results of a data analysis!
How are you using your colors to convey meaning? Chances are that you never thought about this before, but color does convey meaning! If you are using color shades to map different levels of a variable, this implicitly conveys hierarchical order. Another useful rule of thumb to keep in mind is the minimization of the so called data-ink ratio. This means that you want to eliminate any elements from your design that do not express information about your data. The general rule is: If the element does not convey any information over and above what is already present, remove it!

Even though Excel tempts you with a variety of background patterns and colors, these ornaments are often uninformative. 3D charts are another example of unnecessary design elements that inexperienced Excel analysts use and abuse. In fact, for the human eye it is harder to compare dimensions of 3D objects versus 2D objects. Moreover, depth does not convey any additional information to the reader. Most of the time when people use a 3D chart they are showing off… too bad that they are just showing their ignorance of good visual design!
Insights

Data visualization is storytelling
Using color in a meaningful way
Avoid pie-charts
Minimizing the data-ink ratio
Delete all element of your chart that do not provide incremental meaning
Select the appropriate chart type

title: Lesson 2 – Using Functions
text:
Data and Information
The pervasiveness of computers in organizations and our personal lives has produced a remarkable increase in the amount of data surrounding us, and the data that we generate. To put things in perspective, a modern connected car generates 35Gb of data every hour. A couple of years ago Facebook reported that its users were uploading 350 million new photos each day… that’s millions, each day! Data is the encoding of raw facts. Data are the millions of bits produced by your connected car, by a customer shopping on a Website, by any of countless transactional information system operating in organizations today. Information, on the other hand, is data in context – data that was manipulated to become intelligible and understandable. While we are surrounded by data, information is actually scarce because there are few people who can analyze data to create information.
Insights

There is an old principle in data analysis circles: GIGO – Garbage In, Garbage Out. Before analyzing data be sure that it is reliable. Control for measurement, recording, and encoding errors.
Whenever you receive data for analysis get used to performing basic descriptive representations of the data. This process will help you to understand the available attributes and measures available, their formats, and their characteristics. It will also tip you off to potential errors in the data and it will help you avoid GIGO.
Understanding the data from a business perspective will enable you to spot anomalies easily and to produce more robust and reliable insights.
There is no easy formula to become an expert data analyst. But an excitement about the potential of technology, genuine curiosity and the willingness to learn will take you a long way. Great data analysts present the following characteristics:

Technical skill: they are confident in the use of an analytical tool, such as Excel. Furthermore, they have basic mathematics and statistics competencies.
Business knowledge: they are confident in their understanding of the processes that generated the data. Historically, business people did not have any technical expertise and therefore had to rely on technical experts that did not know much about the business operations. This workflow generated many misunderstandings that often led to the production of inaccurate analysis. Today you can perform your own analyses if you know how to use Excel.

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
section: Be Efficient - Get stuff done quickly
text:
Be Efficient - Get stuff done quickly
Being efficient in Excel will save you a significant amount of time over the duration of your career. If you have ever opened Excel you know that the application presents a big canvas of rows and columns to you. This is both a blessing and a curse. On the one hand, you have full control and lots of freedom to perform your analysis. On the other hand, as with any programming task, there many different ways to perform an analysis and it is critical that you spend time up front designing an efficient approach to it. That means that learning Excel is not just about learning where to click, rather it is about learning how to create structured solutions to analytical problems.
Insights

It is likely that if you ask ten different people to perform an analysis in Excel, they will come up with ten different procedures to achieve it. Such variability is possible because Excel provides an incredible number of features – most of which basic users will never hear about. Efficiently using the available instruments in Excel is critical. While you will develop your unique style as an analyst, efficiency should always drive the design of your workbooks. As a general rule, you want to perform analyses that are parsimonious and dynamic.

Your analysis is parsimonious when you minimize the number of calculations and activities.
Your analysis is dynamic when your workbook is not specific to your data but it is robust to data changes. That is, it will not stop working abruptly if the data changes.

A critical differentiator between sophisticated and unsophisticated Excel users is the habit to always use keyboard shortcuts. The earlier you pick up this habit, the better it is. Here are the shortcuts for your platform. Mac Win
There are some important functionalities that are only accessible through shortcuts. In addition to keyboard shortcuts, Excel offers plenty of mouse shortcuts such as auto-fill series and auto columns adjustment. Over the following sessions, we will highlight the most important ones. However, as a general rule, any time that you find yourself performing a repetitive task (like manually input the days of the week) find out the efficient approach. We guarantee you that it exists!
Creating an Excel worksheet is like programming. The idea of a ‘variable’ is central. Variables are the backbone of any program and application, and Excel is no exemption. In simple terms, a variable is a way to store information for later use. Every cell in Excel is basically a variable. You reference it by its column name and row number (e.g., A3).

title: Lesson 1 - Introduction to Formatting
section: Be Effective - Get stuff done right
text:
Be Effective - Get stuff done right
In your professional life, you will often be asked to perform analyses to share with your employees, colleagues or the boss. Creating professional documents is not only visually appealing, but it is also critical for communication. Creating professional documents is critical to convey the results of complex analyses. Well-formatted, professional spreadsheets get your point across – and they look good doing it! As the designer of the workbook you will have great flexibility, it will be your call whether to present data as a plain table, a colorful one, or a compelling graph. Remember at all times that the design choices you make when sharing your analysis will impact your reader’s opinion of your content (and of your abilities).
Insights

Once you share a report with a colleague or boss you lose control of it. It might happen that, especially if it is an important analysis, your colleagues will share it themselves with other colleagues, and your results might end up in a PowerPoint presentation of a different department. If you are good at what you do, then many people will see that. If you are not that good, well, they will know.
Ultimately your peers will judge you based on the quality of what you produce. This means that you need to deliver reliable analyses presented in a manner that is easily understandable by others. Note that, while a quality presentation does not make up for errors in your data and results, a poor presentation devalues the best of insights. Don’t be your own worst enemy by doing sloppy work or by using the wrong instruments.
You might still think that Excel is just an instrument to perform calculations. In this course, you will see that Excel is much more. It is an instrument for ‘Data Storytelling’ and persuasion. When you enter the job market, as a knowledge worker, you will be expected to explain, enlighten, and engage your audience by providing compelling ‘data driven’ stories.

title: Lesson 6 – Comprehensive analysis
text:
Exercise Overview
Objective Practice all the skills you have learned so far in a comprehensive analysis.
Scenario Cousin Anna is really happy with your work… might she get her venture capital firm to invest? Ok, let’s not run too far ahead. In fact, cousin Anna pointed out some mistakes that need your attention. Moreover, it is time to perform unit economics calculations for the whole year of cousin Vinnie pizza franchise data. You should also integrate the insights with you earlier market analysis. This type of joint analysis is required to establish the financial viability of your idea. You have come a long way since you started this project and you have now reached its last step. Be confident in yourself and go for it!

title: Lesson 5 – Summarizing Data with Pivot Tables
text: In this section, we focus on the concept of summarization. We showed previously how visualizing, sorting and filtering data can help you quickly get a sense for what is going on in your data. This preliminary work is important because it enables you to be more efficient in designing your analysis. But we have yet to see Microsoft Excel’s best tool for summarizing data: the Pivot Table. Being able to create meaningful Pivot Tables is one of those skills that separates the truly sophisticated end users from the rest. When the data you have to analyze grows beyond a few hundred rows and ten columns it quickly becomes unfeasible to get a feel for it without summarizing your data efficiently. The Pivot Table is the instrument of choice for this task.

While it may not be important to focus on the presentation and management of your Workbook when making trivial spreadsheets, it is critical to do so when you have external users to whom you present your work (e.g., a boss). In this section, we will first practice the analytical skills we acquired last week, we will then turn our attention on formalizing the process of designing a good Workbook.

title: Lesson 3 – Worksheets and Workbook Management
text:
Readability and Accessibility of Results
We have already introduced the concepts of accessibility and readability in the context of designing Microsoft Word documents. As we said at the time, “you may have never thought about it in these terms, but writing a document is an act of design.” Well, guess what, creating a professional and usable Microsoft Excel Workbook is also an act of design. When sophisticated end-users open Excel to perform some analysis, they have already conceptualized what they want to accomplish – the results of the analysis – and how to accomplish it – the algorithm they develop to analyze the data. We will discuss more fully the process of designing a Worksheet in terms of inputs, processing and outputs, in a later chapter. For now we want to focus on the design of the results sheet. In fact, thinking about who will access the results and what those results should look like is the first step in the design of your Workbook. As with any other report, like the ones you wrote with Microsoft Word, you have to make sure that your results are accessible and readable. In the context of an Excel results page: - Accessibility refers to the visual layout and navigation of your page. You should think about the degree of knowledge your intended audience has about your analysis. If you are sharing your Workbook with your boss for example, you can expect her to be very familiar with the language you use, the data you have processed in your Worksheet, the variables and metrics you use to display your results, the relationships between these metrics, and the objectives of your analysis. For example, if you are working in the finance department of a large Pizza chain, you will likely be processing pizza orders by store, revenues and cost figures, and the like. Your boss would be very familiar with all these. Compare the above situation with one where you are creating a Worksheet that you will be using with potential partners, new employees, investors or customers who are generally unfamiliar with your business. They will likely not have intimate knowledge of your business and you should guide the readers through the analysis. For example, you should make sure to spell out any acronyms, you should visually organize your results in coherent groups, you may consider having a section of the results page with definitions and explanation of metrics. In short, accessibility is about “stepping in your audience’s shoes” and design the visual layout and labeling of your results to be as simple and immediate as possible for them. - Readability refers to the understandability of your results by your intended audience. When thinking about the readability of your results remember what you learned in the context of Microsoft Word. Any element on the results page should have predictable and consistent formatting. You achieve this with the same stylistic elements you have at your disposal in Word. For example, you should select a Theme that will provide consistency of colors and styles to your Worksheet. You should be sure to use consistent formatting for labels and use font size and effects to visually separate them while signaling their relationships – similarly to how you would use styles in Microsoft Word. As in any report, objects and elements should be self-standing. If you have a chart in your results page, you should not assume that the reader knows what it is about. The chart should have a title, the axes should be clearly labeled and have a clear scale and, potentially, a legend. You generally will not use images much, but you may consider having a logo on your results page. While generally what you see on screen is what you get on paper when it comes to Microsoft Word documents, the same cannot be said about Microsoft Excel. Spreadsheets are generally designed for computations on screen and printing to paper is a secondary concern. However, a well designed Worksheet is one where the creator planned for printing by the user. You will often be creating Workbooks for much less sophisticated users (e.g. your boss!) and you should be prepared for them to do things that don’t fully make sense. On that note, you will learn quickly that those unsophisticated users tend to make mistakes and “break” your carefully crafted Workbooks. Thus, you should be able to protect your work and always lock cells that should not be modified.
Insights

Using Themes in Microsoft Excel
Using colors and formatting to guide the reader
Creating self-standing objects on the results page
Protecting Workbooks
Optimizing Workbook design for on screen viewing
Optimizing Workbook design for printing
